title: Localized Excitonic Emission in Wafer-Scale MOCVD-Grown GaSe 2D Nanosheets for Classical and Non-Classical Light Sources
authors: Bhabani Sankar Sahoo, Nils Fritjof Langlotz, Shachi Machchhar, Kartik Gaur, Robin Günkel, Max Bergmann, Naghmeh Ghadghooni, Aris Koulas-Simos, Jürgen Belz, Chirag Chandrakant Palekar, Maximilian Ries, Kerstin Volz, Stephan Reitzenstein, Imad Limame
abstract: Wafer-scale growth of two-dimensional semiconductors remains a key challenge for their integration into photonic technologies. While most studies of two-dimensional semiconductors have focused on transition metal dichalcogenides and their scalable fabrication, comparatively little attention has been given to III-VI monochalcogenides. Here, we report wafer-scale growth of gallium selenide (GaSe) by metal-organic chemical vapor deposition (MOCVD) and investigate its structural and optical properties for visible-range classical and quantum light emission. Two samples with thicknesses ranging from a few monolayers to several micrometers, controlled via the growth time, were investigated. The 30-minute grown sample yields intense, broad photoluminescence spanning 1.7--2.0$\,$eV, whereas the thinner 3-minute sample exhibits discrete narrow emission lines and single-photon emission with $(g^{(2)}(0) = 0.15 \pm 0.10)$. Remarkably, cathodoluminescence mapping reveals pronounced spatial localization of both spectrally narrow and broad emission centers. Together with temperature-dependent power-law analysis and Raman mapping, our results indicate defect-induced emission rather than intrinsic excitonic recombination. These findings establish wafer-scale MOCVD grown 2D GaSe as a platform for classical and non-classical light sources and highlight defect-engineered localization as a route toward scalable quantum photonics.
summary: The study reports the wafer-scale MOCVD growth of GaSe nanosheets on GaP/Si substrates. It shows that by controlling growth time, the material can transition from a broadband classical light source to a high-quality single-photon emitter. This provides a scalable pathway for integrating quantum light sources into silicon-based photonic circuits.
main_problem: The challenge of achieving wafer-scale, controllable growth of 2D semiconductors, specifically III-VI monochalcogenides like GaSe, for integration into scalable classical and quantum photonic technologies.
main_result: The authors demonstrated wafer-scale MOCVD growth of GaSe on GaP/Si that acts as both a broadband classical light source and a high-purity single-photon emitter ($g^{(2)}(0) = 0.15 \pm 0.10$) via defect-induced localization.
method: Metal-organic chemical vapor deposition (MOCVD) was used for growth, followed by structural characterization (AFM, SEM, XRD, TEM) and optical analysis (micro-PL, TRPL, CL mapping, and HBT interferometry).
model_or_system: The physical system consists of 2D Gallium Selenide (GaSe) nanosheets grown epitaxially on GaP/Si (100) substrates, featuring Se-Ga-Ga-Se tetralayers with c-axis orientation.
key_observables: Second-order correlation function $g^{(2)}(0)$, photon lifetime ($	au$), degree of linear polarization (DLP), photoluminescence energy (1.7-2.0 eV), and Raman vibrational modes.
important_parameters: Growth durations of 3 and 30 minutes; temperature range from 4 K to 100 K; laser power density threshold of $10^6$ W/cm$^2$; lattice mismatch $< 3\%$.
main_assumptions: The emission is attributed to defect-induced localization rather than intrinsic excitonic recombination; the growth is assumed to be phase-pure despite minor $	ext{Ga}_2	ext{Se}_3$ traces.
figures_summary: Fig 1: Crystal structure, morphology (AFM/SEM), and Raman spectra. Fig 2: XRD patterns of the heterostructure. Fig 3: Temperature-dependent PL parameters. Fig 4: 30-min sample PL and polarization properties. Fig 5: Experimental spectroscopy setup. Fig 6: Power-dependent PL, temperature-dependent PL, polarization-resolved PL, TRPL decay, and $g^{(2)}(	au)$ correlation.
paper_structure: The paper begins with the MOCVD growth process and structural verification, moves to morphological and phase analysis of different growth regimes, investigates the spatial and spectral origin of light emission via PL and CL, and concludes with the quantum optical characterization of single-photon emission.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant for quantum optics as it demonstrates a scalable, scalable-compatible method for generating non-classical light (single photons) using defect engineering in a 2D material platform.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1 Structural Analysis of GaSe/GaP/Si. a) Side (top) and top (bottom) views of the GaSe crystal structure, generated using VESTA [40]. b) AFM image (2×2 µm2) showing out-of- plane GaSe nanosheets with a height of up to 262 nm, grown on a GaP/Si substrate after a 3-minute growth duration. The nanosheets exhibit preferential growth along the 〈111〉direction of the GaP substrate. c) AFM image (2×2 µm2) showing planar-trigonal GaSe flakes between taller vertical features, obtained after increasing the growth duration to 30 minutes. d) Raman spectra for the samples shown in (b) and (c), exhibiting characteristic vibrational modes of GaSe (highlighted in orange). An additional out-of-plane...

Fig. 2 Spatially resolved Raman analysis of the 3-minute growth sample. a) Display of the integrated intensity of the A1 1g mode of GaSe. The curve was fitted with a Lorentzian lineshape and a 5th-order polynomial background. b) Mean Raman spectra extracted from the regions highlighted in a). Region A corresponds to large integrated peak areas (red curve), while region B corresponds to a weak signal intensity (blue curve).

Fig. 3 Spatially resolved Raman analysis of the 30-minute grown sample. a) Display of the integrated intensity of the 157 cm−1 mode of Ga2Se3. The curve was fitted with a Lorentzian lineshape and a 5th-order polynomial background. The map has been processed with a median denoising filter of 25 %. b) Raman spectra extracted from the linescan highlighted in a). Spectra were chosen such that they alternate between high-intensity and low-intensity regions of the 157 cm−1 mode.

Fig. 4 Room-temperature PL characterization of GaSe samples: a) PL map showing the integrated area of the PL bands of the 3 minute grown GaSe sample. b) PL map showing the integrated area of the PL bands of the 30 minute grown GaSe sample. c) Average PL spectra of the two samples.

Fig. 5 CL characterization of GaSe samples: a) CL intensity map of the 30-minute-grown GaSe sample at low temperature (20 K) with an exposure time of 100 ms. The red square indicates the region from which the spectrum was extracted. b) Extracted CL spectrum at 20 K from the selected area in (a), showing the emission peak of the 30 min-grown sample. c) CL intensity map of the GaSe sample grown for 3 min, with the red square marking the region used for spectral extraction. d) Corresponding extracted CL spectrum at 20 K from the selected region in (c), displaying the emission peak of the 3 min-grown sample. e) Overlaid CL spectra collected at different spots on the 3 min-grown GaSe sample,...
